title: White Noise: Unpredictable Chaos
concept: White noise is a sequence of random numbers that has a constant mean and variance, but zero correlation over time, meaning it contains no learnable pattern.

options.text: Subtracting the previous value from the current value to remove trend.
Calculating the cumulative sum over a rolling window.
Selecting a random subset of features.

exercises.explain: Correct! Differencing removes non-stationary trends by modeling changes.

cards.title: The static screen
cards.body: If you tune an old TV to an empty channel, you see a screen of flickering black and white dots and hear a steady hiss. That hiss is white noise. Each dot is completely random, showing no relation to the dot next to it or the dot that came before. No matter how long you watch, you can't predict the next pattern.

cards.title: White Noise: Pure randomness
cards.body: A time series is **white noise** if it is a sequence of independent, identically distributed random variables. It has a constant mean and constant variance, but **zero autocorrelation** at all lags. It represents pure, unmodelable chaos.

cards.title: A fair coin flip
cards.body: If you flip a fair coin 100 times, the chance of heads is always 50%. If you get 5 heads in a row, the next flip is still a 50% guess. The sequence has zero memory. Trying to forecast white noise is like trying to guess the next coin flip based on the past — impossible.

cards.title: The target for residuals
cards.body: When you fit a time series model (capturing trend and seasonality), the remaining errors (residuals) *must* look like white noise. If your residuals still have patterns, it means your model has left useful information on the table, and you need to keep tuning.

cards.title: White noise is stationary
cards.body: Since white noise bounces around a constant mean with constant variance, it actually meets the mathematical definition of **stationarity**! However, it is a special case: it is stationary but completely **unpredictable** because it has zero autocorrelation.

cards.title: Pattern vs. White Noise
cards.body: Comparing a modelable timeline to pure noise:

left.text:
Has trend or seasonality.
High autocorrelation.
Can be modeled and forecasted.

right.text:
Completely random fluctuations.
Zero autocorrelation.
Impossible to forecast.

exercises.prompt: What is the key characteristic of white noise?

options.text: It consists of random data points with zero autocorrelation, making it impossible to forecast.
It has a strong, predictable upward trend.
It only occurs in yearly seasonal cycles.
It has a Gini impurity of 0.0.

exercises.explain: Correct! Zero autocorrelation means previous points contain no predictive signal for future points.

exercises.prompt: True or false: If your finished forecasting model's residuals (errors) still contain predictable patterns, your model is complete and ready for deployment.
exercises.explain: False! Residual patterns mean the model missed some trend or seasonality. Residuals must look like white noise (pure random error) before you declare the model finished.

exercises.sentence: Although white noise is completely unpredictable, it is technically ___ because its mean and variance are constant over time.
exercises.explain: Since its statistical properties are stable, white noise is a stationary (but unmodelable) series.

exercises.prompt: Which of these is a description of white noise?

options.text: A list of daily stock returns that have a constant variance of 1% but show zero correlation day-to-day.
Sales figures that rise by exactly $10 every Monday.
Temperature readings that peak in summer and bottom in winter.

exercises.explain: Correct! Stable variance but zero chronological correlation is white noise.

exercises.prompt: You fit a linear model to forecast sales. You plot the model's residuals and notice that every Monday, the residuals show a large positive spike. What does this indicate, and what should you do?

options.text: The residuals are not white noise, indicating the model missed a weekly seasonal pattern. You should add a weekly seasonal feature (like a Monday flag) to the model.
The residuals are white noise, indicating the model is complete.
The model has overfit and you should apply Lasso regularization to prune the features.
The data is completely unmodelable and you should delete the database.

exercises.explain: Exactly! If residuals show a predictable repeating pattern, they aren't white noise yet. The model has missed a weekly seasonal effect. By adding a weekly indicator feature, you capture this pattern, driving the residuals down to pure random white noise. Congratulations on completing the Time Series Basics unit! Next, let's learn how we engineer features directly from these timelines!
